explanation: After prop_0 becomes true for the first time, prop_1 must never be true thereafter.

This requirement states that once prop_0 becomes true, prop_1 must never occur from that point onward. In LTL, this is typically expressed with a G (globally) operator, establishing that whenever prop_0 holds, it is always followed globally by not prop_1.

The requirement states that once prop_0 becomes true, it must never be the case that prop_1 is true thereafter. In LTL, this is expressed as: globally, if prop_0 ever becomes true, then from that point onward, prop_1 must always be false.

This statement says that after 'prop_0' becomes true, 'prop_1' can never become true afterwards. In LTL, this is expressed as: globally, if prop_0 becomes true, then globally after that, prop_1 is always false.

Once prop_0 becomes true, prop_1 must never hold from that point onwards. This is equivalent to saying: if at any moment you reach a state where prop_0 holds, then in all future states, prop_1 does not hold. In LTL, this is expressed as G(prop_0 -> G(!prop_1)).

The specification says that if 'prop_0' ever becomes true at some point, then from that point onward, 'prop_1' should never become true. In LTL, this can be captured by stating that globally (always), if 'prop_0' holds at a step, then from that point on, 'prop_1' must always be false.

The statement says that once prop_0 becomes true, it shall not be prop_1 afterwards. In LTL, this is usually modeled as: Once prop_0 is true, from that point on, prop_1 must always be false. This is expressed as: 'If at any point prop_0 is true, then globally from that point, prop_1 is not true.' In LTL: G(prop_0 -> G(!prop_1))

The statement means that after prop_0 becomes true, it is never the case that prop_1 is true at any future point. That is, once prop_0 holds, prop_1 must not happen afterward. This can be captured by stating that either prop_0 never happens (so the requirement is vacuously true), or after the first point where prop_0 is true, it is always the case that prop_1 is not true (G ¬prop_1 holds from that point). This is expressed using the release (V) operator.

The requirement states that after prop_0 becomes true, prop_1 must never occur. In LTL, this can be captured as: once prop_0 has occurred, it is globally the case that not prop_1. Equivalently, before prop_0, no constraint; after prop_0, prop_1 must not occur.
The requirement says once prop_0 occurs, then from that point on, it shall never be prop_1 again. That is, after the first occurrence of prop_0, prop_1 should always be false globally onward.
